title: A gallery you like just posted that they're using AI to write their show descriptions. 👀 What's your gut reaction?

options.label: 🕵️ I check details, which tool are they using and where does the tool's data come from.
😌 I think, "that's a good time saver"
🔍 I'm curious to try the tool myself.

title: 😤 A friend tells you their photo got used to train an AI model without permission. What's your response?

options.label: "That's messed up. Did you look into what you can do about it?"
"Annoying, but hard to avoid unless you're chronically offline."
"Do you know which tool? I kind of want to see how it works. 👀"

title: 😩 You're behind on three emails, a grant deadline, and a form you've been avoiding for two weeks. A friend says "just have AI do it." Your response?

options.label: "Yeah, that's the plan. 🙌"
"I'd rather not, honestly."
"Yes and I'll have it help with drafting my next artwork too. 👀"

title: 🎨 You're stuck on a piece and someone suggests running a rough version through an image generator, just to see what comes back. You...

options.label: Try it that afternoon.
😬 feel a little weird about it and don't.
🙃 would rather spend that time on literally anything else.

title: 🤔 Someone says to you, "at some point you have to just accept this is happening." Do you agree?

options.label: 🔥 No, accepting it is exactly the problem.
😮‍💨 Sure, I'd rather spend my energy elsewhere.
🧐 Kind of, I'd rather understand it than resist it.

title: 🚧 What's the one thing AI never gets to touch?

options.label: 🎨 My craft. All the other stuff is fair game.
🔒 Anything of mine, period, is preferred.
🤷 Nothing, really. I'm curious where the line even is.

title: 💬 A stranger online says your latest piece "lost credibility" because AI was involved somewhere in the process. How does that land with you?

options.label: 😎 That's fine they felt that way, I'm proud of the work.
😅 That couldn't have been my artwork, no AI involved ever.
🙄 Agree with the sentiment.

title: 📋 You're filling out an artist bio for a group show and there's an optional box: "Was any part of this work made using AI?" What goes through your head?

options.label: 🔎 I triple check what the implications are.
⚡ Not sure how I'll answer.
💭 I'm glad they asked.

title: 🙊 A friend asks what you use AI for, expecting you to say "nothing." What's the honest answer?

options.label: 📎 "Not much except admin stuff, to be fair."
🚫 "Nothing now, but I am curious about it."
😏 "I use it for a lot of things."

title: ✨ You finish a piece you're proud of and someone asks, unprompted, "did AI help create that?" How do you feel about being asked that?

options.label: 😊 Fine, if I've used it I'll share that.
😳 A little defensive.
😤 Annoyed they even asked.

title: 🔮 Last one! Pick the sentence closest to how you feel right now:
desc: You're a mystery so far, let's settle this. 🎲

options.label: 🛑 "I want this to slow down until people have agreed to it."
⚖️ "I want this to be useful without becoming my whole personality."
🚀 "I want to test and experiment myself, before I decide anything."

title: 🛡️ Guardian
desc: You want a say, and that's fair. It's your work on the line. 🔥
context:
"My work, my terms."

Your lens
You didn't ask for any of this. You built a practice and a style over years, and machines trained on it without asking first. Consent matters to you, plainly, and "everyone's doing it" was never a good enough reason to let it slide.

You've also learned that no single tool is going to fix this, so you focus on what actually helps: making scraping more expensive, making your terms clear, keeping records of your process, and backing the collective efforts that are winning in courtrooms and legislatures. You know the numbers because you live them: a third of illustrators report losing commissions to AI, and most say opting out on their own barely makes a dent. That's the reason to organize, not to give up. And your reasons for saying no aren't only about copyright. Water, energy, land, and labor are part of it too. A flat no is a fine answer on its own.

The landscape
• Glaze and Nightshade (UChicago) are still maintained and widely used, but treat them as a signal of non-consent, not a guarantee. A 2025-2026 study found simple upscaling can defeat Glaze more than half the time.
• Cloudflare now blocks AI crawlers by default and offers Pay Per Crawl.
• Content Credentials (C2PA) are growing fast: 6,000+ member orgs, built into Leica, Nikon, Canon, Sony cameras and the Pixel 10.
• Bartz v. Anthropic settled for $1.5B+. Andersen v. Stability AI (the visual artists' case) goes to trial September 2026. The NO FAKES Act is advancing in the US Senate.
• The IEA puts data-centre electricity use at about 1.5% of the global total and rising fast.

Quick wins
• Flip the opt-outs that exist: Meta, LinkedIn (Settings > Data Privacy > Data for Generative AI Improvement > off), X, TikTok, and add GPTBot, ClaudeBot, CCBot, Google-Extended to your robots.txt.
• Try Have I Been Trained and register with Spawning. Add an ai.txt file and noai/noimageai meta tags to your site either way.
• Turn on Content Credentials wherever your camera or Adobe workflow supports it.

Medium effort
• Put your site behind Cloudflare and turn on AI-crawler blocking.
• Keep a presence on Cara, the artist-built anti-scraping platform. If you'd rather stay on Instagram, TikTok, or YouTube, use their AI-disclosure toggles paired with a clear caption about your terms.
• Glaze/Nightshade what you post and keep records of your process (layered files, timestamps, process video).

Deep engagement
• Join the Concept Art Association, Human Artistry Campaign, Authors Guild, or your regional body.
• Look into The AI Resist List for the wider organizing landscape, and Data & Society or the AI Now Institute if you want the research behind it.

Key resources
• Glaze / Nightshade
• Have I Been Trained / Spawning
• Cloudflare AI Crawl Control
• Cara
• Content Credentials
• US Copyright Office: AI
• The AI Resist List
• Data & Society / AI Now Institute
• IEA: Energy and AI

Regional notes
• Canada: CARFAC, RAAV, ACTRA
• UK: DACS, Association of Illustrators, Equity, Creators' Rights Alliance
• US: Tennessee ELVIS Act, California AB 1836/2602, NO FAKES Act pending
• EU: GDPR gives you a real right to object; AI Act transparency rules start August 2026

title: 🗂️ Steward
desc: You've drawn a line and it's staying put. AI gets the inbox, never the studio. 💼
context:
"AI for logistics, not my creative work."

Your lens
You're the pragmatist. You didn't become an artist to spend Sunday nights on grant portals and rewriting your bio for the third time, so if a machine wants that job, fine, on your terms. AI runs the back office. It never touches the work.

What actually worries you is more practical: which tools can you trust with your data, and whether a funder will care that you used one. You read the terms of service before you paste anything in. You know one unpublished proposal dropped into a free chatbot can cause problems later. So you keep a simple rule: nothing sensitive leaves the studio without knowing where it's going. Meanwhile your admin time keeps shrinking and your studio time keeps growing, and you're not mad about it.

The landscape
• Funders are on board with limits: Canada Council permits AI for outlines, grammar, and translation, but warns against pasting sensitive data in. Arts Council England allows cautious use and confirms humans always assess applications.
• Automation has moved past drag-and-drop connectors. Claude Cowork, ChatGPT Agent, Gemini's agent mode, and Copilot Tasks can now carry out multi-step admin work directly. Zapier has shifted into the plumbing underneath these agents rather than being the tool itself.
• Business/enterprise tiers of ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot exclude your data from training, which is the level funders themselves recommend.
• Local models (Ollama, LM Studio) run entirely on your machine. Good current picks: Phi-4-mini, Gemma 3, Qwen3.

Quick wins
• Turn off training in every consumer AI tool. One rule: nothing unpublished, financial, or about other people goes into a public chatbot.
• Use AI for grant outlines and plain-language edits, disclose per each funder's rules, and rewrite until it sounds like you.
• Set Google Alerts for your name and project titles.

Medium effort
• Move sensitive drafting to a business/enterprise tier.
• Pick one agent (Claude Cowork, ChatGPT Agent, Gemini, or Copilot Tasks) and hand it one real recurring job, like open-call research or deadline tracking. Start read-only until you trust it.

Deep engagement
• Run a fully local assistant (LM Studio + Phi-4-mini or Gemma 3) for private, zero-cloud drafting.
• Consolidate your studio ops in one system, like Notion, with clear rules about what data lives where.

Key resources
• Canada Council AI guidance
• Arts Council England
• Claude Cowork / ChatGPT Agent / Gemini / Copilot Tasks
• Ollama / LM Studio
• Google Alerts
• Permacomputing

Regional notes
• Canada: Canada Council's FASTER-based guidance is the reference point.
• UK: ACE permits cautious use; the Digital Culture Network offers free tech support.
• US: No blanket funder bans; disclose when in doubt.
• EU: GDPR shapes tool choice; prefer enterprise tiers or local models for personal data.

title: 🚀 Trailblazer
desc: You'd rather find out firsthand than take anyone's word for it. ✨
context:
"The machine is a material."

Your lens
You like trying new things and always have. You'd rather test a tool yourself and see what it can and can't do than read another opinion piece about it. But you're not careless about it. You know using AI can still cost people work or trust in some circles, and you want to be able to explain exactly how you made something if someone asks.

So you're careful about it. You pick models based on where their data came from, the same way you'd think about materials. You'd rather train on your own work than rely on something scraped from elsewhere. You label what you make and keep a record of your part in it, and you don't copy a living artist's style. You're trying to build the kind of practice you'd want to see more of: upfront about consent, clear about process, still recognizably yours. Artists like Holly Herndon and Mat Dryhurst, Refik Anadol, and Claire Silver show that being open about this can work.

The landscape
• Licensed-data models are real options: Adobe Firefly, Bria, and Fairly Trained-certified models all train on consented or licensed data.
• LoRA training on your own work is more accessible than ever, through Kohya_ss, AI-Toolkit, fal.ai, or Replicate.
• The US Copyright Office says prompts alone earn no copyright, but your own drafts, edits, and arrangement decisions do. Keep your process files.
• EU AI Act Article 50 requires machine-readable labeling of AI-generated content starting August 2026.
• The style-licensing economy is emerging: Exactly.ai, Created by Humans, and similar platforms let you license your own style on your terms.

Quick wins
• Default to licensed/indemnified models (Firefly, Bria) for client work. Check Fairly Trained's certified list before adopting new tools.
• Label AI-assisted work everywhere: hashtags, Content Credentials, and the native toggles on Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube.
• Never prompt "in the style of [living artist]." Describe the technique or mood instead.

Medium effort
• Train a LoRA on your own work, locally or through fal.ai/Replicate.
• Build an authorship archive: prompts, drafts, layers, edit history. It's what makes hybrid work registrable.
• Register the human-authored parts of significant work with the Copyright Office, disclosing AI use per current guidance.

Deep engagement
• License your style on your own terms through Exactly.ai or similar platforms; pursue Fairly Trained certification if you ship a model.
• Prepare for EU AI Act labeling if your work reaches EU audiences.
• Work in the open: process posts, honest credits, model cards for anything you train.

Key resources
• Fairly Trained
• Adobe Firefly
• Bria
• Kohya_ss / AI-Toolkit / fal.ai
• US Copyright Office: AI
• Content Credentials
• EU AI Act Article 50
• Exactly.ai

Regional notes
• US: No copyright for pure AI output, document your human contribution.
• UK/Canada: Human authorship required.
• EU: Article 50 labeling required from August 2026.
• Global: Rules genuinely differ by country, check where you sell.
